items.title: Neonatal screen for cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Diagnosis by neonatal screen

  • The neonatal screen measures immunoreactive trypsinogen in dried blood; a value > 99th percentile requires repeating the test.
  • If it persists elevated, a genetic study (mutation panel) and sweat chloride test are performed as confirmation.
  • False positives: prematurity, perinatal stress, kidney failure; false negatives: mild mutations with residual function of the cystic fibrosis transmembrane conductance regulator (CFTR).
  • In newborns with meconium ileus, the screen may be normal; sweat test and direct genetics must be done.

items.title: F508del mutation in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Most frequent F508del mutation

  • The F508del mutation (phenylalanine deletion in codon 508) is the most prevalent (≈70% of alleles in the Caucasian population).
  • It produces a misfolded CFTR protein that does not reach the apical membrane; it is degraded in the endoplasmic reticulum.
  • Clinic: classic phenotype with exocrine pancreatic insufficiency from birth and progressive lung disease.
  • F508del homozygotes have a worse prognosis; compound heterozygotes with residual function mutations may have milder disease.

items.title: Sweat chloride test
items.contentHtml:
Chloride in sweat diagnostic test

  • Gold standard: iontophoresis with pilocarpine (stimulates sweating); ≥15 µL of sweat is collected.
  • Diagnostic threshold: chloride ≥60 mmol/L in two separate samples confirms cystic fibrosis.
  • Intermediate values ​​(30–59 mmol/L) require genetic study and clinical evaluation; <30 mmol/L is normal.
  • False positives: edema, malnutrition, atopic dermatitis; false negatives: insufficient sweat, atypical mutations.

items.title: Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Exocrine pancreatic insufficiency

  • Present in ≈85% of patients; caused by obstruction of pancreatic ducts due to thick secretions.
  • Clinic: steatorrhea (voluminous, smelly, fatty stools), weight loss, deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
  • Diagnosis: fecal elastase <200 µg/g (normal >200); also fecal fat test in 72 hours.
  • Treatment: pancreatic enzymes (lipase 500–4000 U/g of ingested fat) with each meal; adjust according to control of steatorrhea and growth.

items.title: Meconium ileus
items.contentHtml:
Meconium ileus in newborn

  • It is the earliest neonatal presentation (≈10–15% of cases); intestinal obstruction due to thick meconium in the distal ileum.
  • Clinic: abdominal distention, bilious vomiting, absence of meconium emission in the first 24–48 hours.
  • X-ray: dilated loops, air-fluid levels, absence of air in the rectum; intestinal pneumatosis may occur if complicated.
  • Treatment: enema with N-acetylcysteine (mucolytic) or water-soluble contrast; if it fails, surgery (enterotomy with lavage).

items.title: Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Pseudomonas aeruginosa infection

  • Main pathogen in chronic lung disease; forms biofilm in the airway, resistant to antibiotics.
  • Early acquisition worsens prognosis; It is classified into: initial infection (eradicable) and chronic colonization (mucoid phenotype).
  • Treatment of first infection: oral ciprofloxacin + inhaled tobramycin (300 mg every 12 hours) for 28 days.
  • In acute exacerbation: ceftazidime or piperacillin-tazobactam intravenous, associated with aminoglycoside.

items.title: Hypercapnia and chronic hypoxemia in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Chronic hypercapnia and hypoxemia

  • Progress with obstructive and restrictive lung damage; CO2 retention (blood pressure of carbon dioxide >45 mmHg) indicates advanced disease.
  • Hypoxemia (oxygen saturation <90%) at rest or during sleep is criteria for home oxygen therapy.
  • Chronic hypercapnia can lead to cor pulmonale (right ventricular hypertrophy) due to pulmonary vasoconstriction hypoxic.
  • Evaluation: arterial blood gas, nocturnal oximetry, echocardiogram to rule out pulmonary hypertension.

items.title: Pneumatosis intestinalis and distal intestinal obstruction syndrome
items.contentHtml:
Pneumatosis intestinalis and distal obstruction

  • Distal intestinal obstruction syndrome (DIOS) is obstruction of the terminal ileum by thick feces; Do not confuse with meconium ileus (neonatal).
  • Clinic: abdominal pain, palpable mass in the right iliac fossa, vomiting; X-ray shows levels and intestinal pneumatosis (gas in the intestinal wall) in severe cases.
  • Risk factors: untreated exocrine pancreatic insufficiency, dehydration, use of opioids.
  • Treatment: hydration, enema with N-acetylcysteine or water-soluble contrast; if it does not resolve, oral polyethylene glycol or intestinal lavage.

items.title: Obstructive azoospermia in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Obstructive azoospermia in men

  • Present in >95% of men with cystic fibrosis; It causes infertility due to congenital bilateral absence of vas deferens.
  • The vas deferens atrophy during embryonic development due to dysfunction of the CFTR in the mesonephros.
  • Diagnosis: azoospermia in semen, low seminal volume (acidic pH), absence of vas deferens on transrectal ultrasound.
  • Do not confuse with secretory azoospermia (testicular failure); Here spermatogenesis is normal, so assisted fertility (testicular sperm extraction) is possible.

items.title: Deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins

  • Consequence of exocrine pancreatic insufficiency and poor absorption of fats; affects vitamins A, D, E, K.
  • Vitamin A: xerophthalmia, night blindness; vitamin D: rickets, osteoporosis; vitamin E: peripheral neuropathy, hemolytic anemia; Vitamin K: Coagulopathy (prolonged prothrombin time). vitamin E 100–400 IU/day, vitamin K 1–5 mg/day (oral).

items.title: Nasal polyposis and chronic sinusitis in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Nasal polyposis and chronic sinusitis

  • Frequent otorhinolaryngological manifestation (≈30–50%); Nasal polyps are inflammatory, not allergic, and tend to recur after surgery.
  • Chronic sinusitis is almost universal in cystic fibrosis; thick secretions obstruct the sinus ostia.
  • Microbiology: paranasal sinus culture usually isolates Pseudomonas aeruginosa or Staphylococcus aureus.
  • Treatment: nasal washes with hypertonic saline solution, intranasal corticosteroids, antibiotics (oral or topical) according to culture; endoscopic surgery if severe obstruction.

items.title: CFTR modulators in cystic fibrosis
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with CFTR modulators

  • Drugs that correct the CFTR protein defect: ivacaftor (enhancer), lumacaftor, tezacaftor and elexacaftor (correctors).
  • Main indication: patients with at least one copy of the F508del mutation. (elexacaftor/tezacaftor/ivacaftor is the most effective regimen).
  • Benefits: improvement in forced expiratory volume in the first second (FEV1), reduction of pulmonary exacerbations, weight gain and decrease in chloride in sweat.
  • Adverse effects: elevation of transaminases, cataract (in children), interaction with cytochrome P450 inducers (avoid rifampicin, carbamazepine).
